item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd like to see an explanation as to how they found out about the properties of the material, i.e. that it consists of exotic matter instead of quarks.

The "exotic matter" is just another fancy of saying "this stuff is really unique" and "we actually have no clue what this thing is made of."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This had one of those patented "Oh shit" moments that makes for a compelling SCP. A clever concept, well executed. Plus one!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd like to see an explanation as to how they found out about the properties of the material, i.e. that it consists of exotic matter instead of quarks.

IRL, you can only observe the outward behavior of objects as small as a proton, so to see what it's made of, you make it collide at a high velocity and observe the outward behavior of all the particles coming out of it, but that obviously is not an option for this case as they don't have a reproducable source for this sort of particle, otherwise it couldn't be called an anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Obligatory PBF

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The perfect mix of scientific grounding and fantastical impossibility. This really is a classic SCP article with a modern-day polish. Have your 200th upvote, you've earned it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …so below

item.title: Re: I Like
item.content:encoded: It was produced by scp-914, presumably on very fine, so it probably isn’t made of ordinary matter

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: damn this is good

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded:
+ Open
- Sesame

I really like this, but I just can't get over the fact that they found this one, singular particle, without the complete destruction of the planet.

I already explained my thoughts on how they'd find this in the first page of this discussion. Basically, they'd detect the frequency of Cosmic background radiation emanating from somewhere within their site. It serves no purpose to explain why they'd know about it in the context of a scientific documentation of the anomaly.

If that was the needle in the testing log, then no one or nothing collided with it in the entirety of its existence?

I'm trying to understand what you just said since it's worded a bit off, but yes, it didn't hit anything, and even if it did? That wouldn't really affect the universe that much by something so long and thin.

Also, I don't see anything in the log about what would be a pretty blatant Lifted Veil Scenario. A giant object just starts coming at Earth, stops, disappears, and there's no backlash? No one said anything?

The Foundation has technology decades ahead of current technological developments. I'm very certain that their satellites would know if something was headed straight for them before any publicly commercial satellite would.

I try to suspend my disbelief when reading these articles, but this one…just the sheer likelihood of the Foundation finding it is so minute, it is practically zero.

Uh… no. It really isn't. The Foundation has tech designed to contain aberrations that break the laws of physics, reality, and even logic on a daily basis. Don't you think the Foundation would need really, and I mean really, advanced tech to compensate for that?

This article is sitting at nearly 150+ (at the time of writing this comment) and is upvoted by a good majority among well-respected authors and lurkers alike. I'm pretty good at where it is right now.

I really like this, but I just can't get over the fact that they found this one, singular particle, without the complete destruction of the planet. If that was the needle in the testing log, then no one or nothing collided with it in the entirety of its existence? Also, I don't see anything in the log about what would be a pretty blatant Lifted Veil Scenario. A giant object just starts coming at Earth, stops, disappears, and there's no backlash? No one said anything?

I try to suspend my disbelief when reading these articles, but this one…just the sheer likelihood of the Foundation finding it is so minute, it is practically zero.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Simple, sweet, and to the point. Not gonna lie, I love a good recursion SCP, and anything with the title "as above, so below," or something like it catches my attention. Easy +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy shit, aren't you the guy that made SCP-294?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As soon as I saw radio waves I was like "this is Horton Hears a Who". +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "What the fuck is that fucking needle looking thing doing in the air?"

item.title: I Like
item.content:encoded:
I very much enjoyed reading this Skip.
Imagining watching the faces of researchers as realisation set in put a smile on my face.
The only issue I have is the meshing of quantum and macro size scales. I.E a needle (no matter how well refined) interacting with a single hydrogen nucleus, one of the smallest instances of matter in the universe. The weak and strong forces alone…… ahem.
I know I know there is no story without it, and I dont really have a solution to put forward, I realise im being pedantic, I just can't ignor the discrepancy. But better than anything I could ever produce so
Still gets an upvote from me.
GG

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not a molecule, it's the size of a subatomic particle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Miniature Earth-molecule that replicates the effect on the actual Earth. Got it. +1

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: Deal.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: As the person who contributed the upvote which tipped the score over I expect a share of all royalties.
Who knew that my tenth scip would be the first to reach 100 upvotes?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: some days at the SCPF, you just can't help but crash-land on your own helmet, and you can only pray you find your bald spot isn't getting worse

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really simple and well showcased. Maybe we have to use the suspension of disbelief a bit too much, but isn't that what this site is about most of the time?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So wait, are we on the atom? But how do we threaten ourselves? Are we superseding us now? Is there A Voice Behind Us??

+1 for planetwide existential crisis.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a textbook SCP, in my opinion. Fairly short, quirky, but contains an extremely bizarre twist that makes you question everything once you've finished. Great stuff!

“Subatomic particles do not just sit around being subatomic particles. They are beehives of activity.”

- Gary Zukav
Definitely didn't expect the twist ending with the huge space needle. Now i'm just sitting here wondering how the rest of the world didn't notice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wasn't sure where this was leading, definitely wasn't expecting *that*. +1, just for imagining the panic of the researchers once they realized what's what.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
planet.jpg

Holy hell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Foundation needs to make something smaller than an atom to reach SCP-4192. I don't think they could ever make anything on a subatomic scale.

I'm pretty sure that they tested that needle before using it in an experiment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure why the foundation would involve 914 in the testing, since the outputs are seemingly random and you can't be sure what the refined needle would do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Funny skip, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I wanted to make a discovery log that the Foundation managed to pinpoint the location of the atom because they were detecting some weird radio signals emanating from somewhere in its own unused containment chamber.

I admit that it's like finding a really specific needle in a haystack of needles, but that's just my headcanon that the Foundation has tech that's about 50 years more advanced than the current bleeding-edge tech of today to cope with the physics-breaking anomalies that would allow them to detect the radio signals.

That, and it would've made for a pretty short and boring discovery log :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How would you even find this thing?

Like, how do you find a literal atom on a planet? -1, I just can't see any plausible explanation on how the Foundation found this particular particle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 I really enjoyed it! I hope the best for this!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As above, so below. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aw shucks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You use all the right ingredients here for some solid flash fiction: a build-up that's snappy and a payoff with extreme consequences. Nicely done, +1

(Also: Nice character name!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Twist on this one is well executed, especially given the short length.

One minor nitpick, the following broke my flow of reading for a moment:

does not appear to be composed of quarks but of something else entirely

Just sounded a bit casual. Maybe go with “… but of unidentified exotic matter”. That could just be me though, so feel free to ignore.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: Thanks! I tend to write a lot of Series I-esque scips so I was a little worried that this one wouldn't go so well.

Holy crap

I… yeah. As mentioned above this calls to mind Series 1. Actually, it feels like a fusion of Series 1 and 5. Nice work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Everyone needs a little Series I in their lives.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has the vibe of one of them good Series I articles.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
I'm surprised that nobody's made an article about subatomic particles. This should be a first.

coldposted, but thanks to anyone that read this before.

Source of the image.
